{{Short description="Statistical description of identical integer-spin particles}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Bose–Einstein statistics
| image = BE distribution.png
| caption = The Bose–Einstein distribution for various temperatures.
| topics = [[Statistical mechanics]], [[Boson]], [[Identical particles]], [[Quantum mechanics]]
}}

'''Bose–Einstein statistics''' describes the statistical behaviour of [[identical particles|identical]] [[boson]]s — particles with integer [[spin (physics)|spin]] — at [[thermal equilibrium]]. Developed by [[Satyendra Nath Bose]] and [[Albert Einstein]] in 1924–1925, it predicts that, unlike [[fermion]]s, multiple bosons can occupy the same [[quantum state]].

== Bose–Einstein distribution ==
The average occupation number of a state with energy <math>\varepsilon</math> is:

:<math>\langle n(\varepsilon) \rangle = \frac{1}{e^{(\varepsilon - \mu)/k_B T} - 1}</math>

where:
* <math>\mu</math> is the [[chemical potential]] (<math>\mu \leq 0</math> for bosons)
* <math>k_B</math> is the [[Boltzmann constant]]
* <math>T</math> is the [[temperature]]

== Properties ==
* <math>\langle n \rangle</math> can be arbitrarily large — no exclusion principle
* As <math>\mu \to 0</math> (or <math>T</math> decreases), occupation of the ground state diverges → [[Bose–Einstein condensation]]
* At high <math>T</math> or low density, reduces to [[Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics]]

== Bose–Einstein condensation ==
{{Main|Bose–Einstein condensate}}
Below a critical temperature <math>T_c</math>, a macroscopic fraction of bosons occupies the ground state:

:<math>T_c \approx \frac{2\pi\hbar^2}{m k_B}\left(\frac{n}{\zeta(3/2)}\right)^{2/3}</math>

This state of matter was first experimentally realised in 1995 by [[Eric Cornell]], [[Carl Wieman]], and [[Wolfgang Ketterle]] ([[Nobel Prize in Physics|Nobel Prize]] 2001).

== Applications ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! System !! Phenomena
|-
| [[Photon]] gas || [[Black-body radiation]] spectrum (Planck's law)
|-
| [[Phonon]]s || [[Heat capacity]] of solids at low temperature
|-
| [[Cooper pair]]s || [[Superconductivity]]
|-
| [[Helium-4]] || [[Superfluidity]] below 2.17 K
|-
| Ultracold atoms || [[Bose–Einstein condensate]]
|-
| [[Laser]] || Stimulated emission ↔ bosonic enhancement
|}

== Photon statistics and Planck's law ==
Photons are bosons with <math>\mu = 0</math>. The Bose–Einstein distribution gives [[Planck's law]]:

:<math>\langle n \rangle = \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}</math>

== Contrast with Fermi–Dirac statistics ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! !! Bose–Einstein !! Fermi–Dirac
|-
| Spin || Integer || Half-integer
|-
| Occupation || Unlimited || ≤1 per state
|-
| Distribution || <math>1/(e^{(E-\mu)/k_B T} - 1)</math> || <math>1/(e^{(E-\mu)/k_B T} + 1)</math>
|}

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Boson]]
* [[Fermi–Dirac statistics]]
* [[Identical particles]]
* [[Statistical mechanics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Statistical mechanics]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Particle physics]]
